Singapore University of Technology and Design

50.041: Distributed Systems & Computing

Team 6 Project Proposal

---

**PeerSync: Autonomous Resource Allocation in GPU Render Farms via Synchronised Reputation Ledgers and Strong Leader Consensus**

---

| *Authors:* | *Student ID:* |
| :---- | :---- |
| Elliot Phua | 1006967 |
| Avitra Phon | 1006946 |
| Benjamin Teoh | 1007197 |

March 25, 2026

## Inspiration
https://github.com/abdelmounaime/Maekawa
https://github.com/sanketc/MaekawaAlgorithm 

## 1\. Introduction & Overall Functionalities

This project develops a distributed resource orchestration system for managing task assignments across heterogeneous compute providers. It models a marketplace where requesters submit jobs and worker nodes provide computational resources, focusing on coordination, fault tolerance, and consistent state management.

Inspired by Kubernetes and decentralized coordination models, the system addresses scalable orchestration of large-scale workloads (e.g. rendering, AI) without centralized bottlenecks.

The system consists of two layers: a **Resource Orchestration layer and a Consensus Cluster** layer, separating execution from control. In the **Resource Orchestration layer**, workers coordinate task allocation via a quorum-based protocol inspired by Maekawa’s algorithm, where the worker that obtains quorum executes the task, preventing conflicts.

**The Consensus Cluster layer,** implemented using Raft, serves as the source of truth. It maintains a replicated log of key events, with all updates committed through consensus to ensure global consistency and fault tolerance.

## 2\. Logical Components & Requirements

### 2.1 System Architecture

![][image2]

| Layer | Description |
| ----- | ----- |
| Communication Layer | Peer-to-peer TCP \+ RPC for Raft messages (RequestVote, AppendEntries, heartbeats) and task coordination |
| Ledger Layer | Replicated log storing system events; entries committed after majority agreement |
| Consensus Layer | Raft handles leader election, log replication, quorum, and recovery |
| Execution Layer | State machine applies committed entries (task assignment, slashing, reputation, pricing) |
| Fault-Tolerance Layer | Heartbeats and timeouts trigger leader re-election to maintain liveness |

### 

### 2.2 Logical Components

| Component | Distributed Concept | Logic / Algorithm |
| ----- | ----- | ----- |
| Term Manager | Clock & Ordering | **Raft terms** ensure ordered leadership epochs and reject stale leaders |
| Market Leader | Strong Leadership | Leader appends **final state updates**; serializes global outcome |
| Election Engine | Leader Election | **Randomized timeouts \+ RequestVote RPCs** for majority election |
| Log Replicator | Consensus | **AppendEntries RPC** replicates committed events; majority → final |
| Heartbeat Monitor | Fault Tolerance | Missing heartbeats trigger re-election |
| Worker Coordinator | Distributed Mutual Exclusion | **Quorum-based coordination (Maekawa)** for task ownership |
| Economic Enforcer | State Machine | Applies committed entries (status, reputation, rewards, penalties, pricing) |

### 2.3 Potential Technical Challenges

* **Split vote**: simultaneous elections → resolved via randomized timeouts  
* **Log divergence**: inconsistent logs after crash → resolved by leader overwrite  
* **Ghost leader**: outdated leader → resolved via term validation  
* **State machine lag**: inconsistent application → resolved via commit index

Quorum-based coordination introduces additional challenges:

* **Deadlock**: overlapping quorum requests → implement yield/inquire messages; alternatively mitigated via timeouts or priority ordering  
* **Starvation**: repeated quorum failure → mitigated via fairness policies  
* **Conflicting ownership**: network delay → resolved by treating quorum as proposals and finalizing via Raft
